fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to wrap up the zonal extraction tool we've been building. the core engine is mostly done but we're getting complaints from a few data science folks that the output tables are inconsistent depending on how they pass in polygons and rasters. basically the ask is: given a set of polygon features and one or more raster grids, we should be able to spit out a clean CSV (or shapefile in some cases) with whatever summary numbers they need per polygon. things like averages, most common value, spread of categories, that kind of stuff. there's also a bunch of edge cases around empty intersections and missing data that keep biting people — same kind of handling we did for the nodata sentinel logic in the reprojection module, just make sure that's consistent here too. one tricky bit: when someone only has a single unnamed raster, the column headers in the output should just use the operation name directly instead of inventing some raster prefix. also cell-level outputs need to work where each intersecting cell gets its own row. and there's some metadata about scaling/offsets on raster bands that needs to be applied before values go out. the identifier column in output should support renaming and type-casting. geometry passthrough to shapefiles should be supported when requested. please make sure fractional overlap is exact and not just a nearest-cell approximation — that was a big complaint last time.

one small follow-up from the questions that came in: the unnamed-raster column naming rule is intentionally narrow. when exactly one raster is supplied and that raster has no name field, stats that don’t explicitly reference a raster should use just the operation name, like 'mean' or 'variety', instead of building a 'raster_operation' style name. that only applies when the raster is unnamed AND there is exactly one raster in the input. if the raster has a name, the compound prefix form is always used.

also, on overlap math, we do mean exact fractional area of overlap for every intersecting cell, not a simple intersects flag and not centroid containment. so if a cell is 25% covered, it contributes 0.25 to weighted aggregates like mean. and if someone asks for 'coverage' at cell level, that stat should expose that exact fraction directly on each row.

for nodata / empty intersections, please keep the behavior aligned with the existing logic: integer rasters use the configured 'nodata' field value as the sentinel mask, float rasters treat NaN as missing, and when all intersecting cells are missing the mode behavior depends on dtype. for an integer raster with all cells masked by the sentinel, mode returns the nodata sentinel itself, like -999, NOT nan. for float rasters where NaN is the missing value, mode should return nan. count and mean always return 0 and nan respectively when there are no valid observations, regardless of dtype.

one more detail on 'frac': the schema expands across the whole batch, not feature by feature. we emit one column per category observed across ALL features in the batch, named 'frac_<category_value>', and sort those columns by category value ascending. if a feature doesn’t contain a given category, that spot in the CSV should be an empty field, not zero and not nan, literally empty. and if a category appears in no feature, it should not be in the schema at all.

last thing, the cell-level stats are 'coverage', 'values', 'cell_id', and 'centroid_x'/'centroid_y'. if any of those are requested, output switches to one row per intersecting raster cell per feature instead of one summary row per feature, and all requested cell-level columns need to line up on the same per-cell rows. 'cell_id' is the linear raster index of the cell in row-major order, zero-based, and it should be output as a quoted string. rows should be ordered by cell_id ascending.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def process(features, rasters, statistics, options, output_format=None, feature_srs=None) -> str

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a raster definition includes 'scale' and/or 'offset' fields, physical values must be computed as: physical = raw * scale + offset before being reported in any output (values, mean, quantile, etc.). This transformation is applied after nodata masking but before all statistical aggregation and cell-level emission. Rasters without scale/offset fields are passed through unchanged.

The options block may contain 'identifier_output_name' (a string) to rename the identifier column header in the CSV output, and 'identifier_output_type' ('float', 'int', or 'string') to cast the identifier value before writing. When cast to float or int, the value is not quoted in the CSV. When kept as string or default, the value is quoted. The feature's identity value itself is preserved; only presentation changes.

When 'output_format' is 'shp' and 'include_geometry' is true in options, the output should include the original polygon geometry and assign the spatial reference given by 'feature_srs' (an integer EPSG code). The expected output format for tests is: 'fields=<col>=<val>;...' on one line, 'geometry_type=<TYPE>' on the next, and 'srs_contains_vermont=true/false' on the third line when the SRS covers Vermont (EPSG:32145 does). Statistics requested with 'output': 'y' use that alias as the column name in output.

For quantile statistics, the output column name includes the probability expressed as an integer percentage. For example, q=0.25 produces a column suffix '_quantile_25', q=0.1 produces '_quantile_10'. When the raster is named, the full column is '<raster>_quantile_<pct>'; when implicit single raster, it is 'quantile_<pct>'. The q parameter is required in the statistic definition for quantile operations.

The 'copy_attributes' option accepts a list of feature property names (excluding geometry and identifier) to be copied verbatim into every output row for the corresponding feature. In the CSV header and rows, the order is: identifier column first, then copy_attributes in the order listed, then computed statistic columns. Attribute values that are integers are quoted in CSV output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The identifier formatting rules in feature8_identifier_field_formatting.json: identifier_output_name renames the CSV header column, identifier_output_type controls Python type casting and CSV quoting behavior (float/int = unquoted numeric, string/default = quoted string value).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or look at how the existing statistics options are structured in the PRD? That should have the exact behavior we need.

feature1_zonal_summary_statistics.json.description: Compute multiple summary statistics for a polygon over a named raster and preserve the identifier column.

feature2_implicit_single_raster_statisti.description: Compute statistics with implicit operation names when exactly one single-band raster is supplied.

feature3_category_fraction_columns.json.description: Return one fraction column per observed raster category across all processed features.

feature4_empty_and_nodata_intersections..description: Report neutral numeric results for features outside the raster or inside cells with no valid values.

feature5_attribute_passthrough.json.description: Copy selected non-geometric feature attributes into every output row alongside computed statistics.

feature6_cell_level_outputs.json.description: Emit one output row per intersecting cell when cell-level statistics are requested.

feature7_scaled_raster_cells.json.description: Apply stored scale and offset metadata before returning raster cell values.

feature8_identifier_field_formatting.jso.description: Rename or type-convert the output identifier field while preserving the feature identity value.

feature9_geometry_and_spatial_reference_.description: Preserve input geometry and spatial reference when geometry output is requested for a geometry-capable output format.
